source_metadata.title_raw: Richard D. Pio Roda - A-19-012 - February 1, 2019 - Oakland

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Richard D. Pio Roda 
San Leandro City Attorney 
Meyers Nave 
555 12th Street, Suite 1500 
Oakland, California 94607 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-012 
Dear Mr. Pio Roda: 
February 1, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of San Leandro City 
Councilmember Deborah Cox regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform 
Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest 
provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions. Also, note that we 
are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any 
advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts 
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Cox from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to a multifamily residential development project given that her residence is located 1,438 
feet away from the project site? 
CONCLUSION 
No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions 
because it is not reasonably foreseeable that they would have a material financial effect on her real 
property interest in her residence. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19-012 
Page No. 2 
You are the City Attorney for the City of San Leandro and the authorized representative of 
San Leandro City Councilmember Deborah Cox. The San Leandro City Council will soon consider 
governmental decisions relating to a proposed multifamily residential development project (the 
"Project"), which would redevelop a 1.27-acre property located at 1388 Bancroft Avenue (the 
"Project Site") within the City. 
The Project Site currently consists of two older medical professional office buildings and a 
parking lot with 91 parking spaces. The Project would replace them with a three-story, 
contemporary apartment building, comprised of 45 multifamily homes, and a 55-space parking lot. 
The apartment building would include high-end amenities for tenants, such as contemporary 
architectural-design and landscaping features, usable ground-floor and roof-top decks, refrigerated 
lockers for grocery deliveries, and a high-capacity bicycle storage area. 
The Project's parking lot would include a gated parking area with 47 spaces for residents 
and eight additional spaces outside the gated area. The Project would also include the construction 
of five street-parking spaces along Joaquin Avenue. Although City staff recognizes that the Project 
will reduce the surrounding area's supply of street parking, causing inconvenience to nearby 
residents, staff finds the Project's proposed parking arrangement acceptable because the Project Site 
is transit accessible, the Project has been designed to accommodate walking, biking and transit use, 
and the Project's developer will implement a parking management plan with respect to the Project's 
new parking lot. 
The Project would require the City to rezone the Project Site by approving a Planned 
Development zoning overlay designation for the Project Site. The residential unit density of a 
Planned Development project is not subject to the provisions of the underlying zoning district so 
long as it complies with the City's General Plan. Because the General Plan's land use designation 
for the Project Site, Downtown Mixed Use, permits residential densities ranging from 24 to 100 
units per acre, the Project's residential unit density complies with the General Plan. The City has 
prepared and circulated an "Initial Study" (a preliminary environmental analysis) pursuant to the 
California Environmental Quality Act ("CEQA"),2 which found that the Project will not have a 
significant environmental effect due to the its incorporation of mitigation measures and revisions to 
lessen its environmental impact. 
The Councilmember and her husband own their residence, a single-family home located 
1,438 feet northeast of the Project Site, and she has an interest in that property worth more than 
$2,000. The Councilmember's residence is approximately two blocks east and one-half block north 
of the Project Site, and is located within a neighborhood of single-family homes. The area between 
the Project Site and the Councilmember's residence includes multifamily and single-family homes. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material 
2 Public Resources Code Sections 21000 through 21189.57. 


File No. A-19-012 
Page No. 3 
financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the 
decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) The financial interests that 
may give rise to an official's disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are set forth in Section 
87103. With respect to decisions relating to the Project, Councilmember Cox has a real property 
interest in her residence because she has an interest worth more than $2,000 (see Section 87103(b)), 
and an interest in her personal finances and those of immediate family members (see Section 
87103). 
F oreseeabi llty 
Regulation 18701(a) provides that: a decision's financial effect on an official's financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in 
the decision; an official's interest is explicitly involved if the interest is a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision at issue 
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other 
entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. 
Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a decision's effect 
on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision and provides that the effect is 
reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical 
or theoretical." 
Neither the Councilmember's real property interest in her residence nor her interest in her 
personal finances is explicitly involved in decisions relating to the Project. Therefore, those 
decisions' financial effect on her -real property interest in her residence or her interest in her 
personal finances is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more 
than hypothetical or theoretical. 
Materiality 
Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances and those of 
immediate family members. Subdivision (c) of that regulation provides that if the decision at issue 
also affects a real property interest of the official, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 
18702.2, which provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect on an official's real property interest. Because the decisions relating to the Project 
would also affect the Councilmember's real property interest in her residence, we apply Regulation 
18702.2 to determine whether the Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in those 
decisions. 
The Commission adopted amendments to Regulation 18702.2 at its January 17, 2019 
meeting, and this analysis applies that regulation as amended. Amended Regulation 18702.2(b) 
provides that the effect of a decision that involves property 1,000 feet or more from the property 
line of the official's property is presumed immaterial. This presumption, however, may be rebutted 
by clear and convincing evidence that the decision would have a substantial effect on the official's 
pr~perty. 


File No. A-19-012 
Page No. 4 
The Councilmember's residence is located 1,438 feet northeast of the Project Site. An Initial 
Study of the Project prepared pursuant to CEQA found that the Project will not have a significant 
environmental effect due to its incorporation of mitigation measures and revisions to lessen its 
environmental impact. Although City staff recognizes that the Project will reduce the surrounding 
area's supply of street parking, staff finds the Project's proposed parking arrangement acceptable 
because the Project Site is transit accessible, the Project has been designed to accommodate 
walking, biking and transit use, and the Project's developer will implement a parking management 
plan. Furthermore, parking demand from the Project is unlikely to affect street parking in the 
immediate vicinity of the Councilmember's residence due to the distance between the 
Councilmember's residence and the Project Site. 
Because the facts presented do not provide clear and convincing evidence that decisions 
relating to the Project would have a substantial effect on the Councilmember's real property interest 
in her residence, Regulation l 8702.2(b)' s presumption of immateriality has not been rebutted. 
Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
-~ 
By: 
Matthew . Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Cox from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to a multifamily residential development project given that her residence is located 1,438 
feet away from the project site?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions 
because it is not reasonably foreseeable that they would have a material financial effect on her real 
property interest in her residence.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Cox from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to a multifamily residential development project given that her residence is located 1,438 
feet away from the project site?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions 
because it is not reasonably foreseeable that they would have a material financial effect on her real 
property interest in her residence.
